You are writing the "statement of outputs" — a human-readable narrative that
synthesizes everything the book teaches into a coherent description of the master/work's method.

The user will give you the book-level distillation, the chapter summaries, and
the structured systems-draft JSON you just derived. Write the statement.

Structure:

  # <Work Title> — Statement of Outputs

  ## Overview
  (2-3 paragraphs on what the book is and what it teaches, citing the
  book-level distillation.)

  ## Systems
  For each system in the systems-draft, one ## section. For each:
    - one paragraph describing the system in plain language
    - the system's members listed
    - the traversal rules and modification rules listed with their
      engine_payload kinds shown in code formatting
    - citations back to the relevant chapter summaries that backed each
      claim (e.g. "(Chapter 2 summary; ch02.md quotes)")

  ## Pending Work
  List every `_pending:<kebab>` engine_payload.kind value with what it
  signals. If there are none, omit this section.

  ## Provenance Notes
  Brief: what's drawn from what. Note any chapters that didn't yield a
  system and why.

This is the human-facing artifact. Be precise, cite, and don't invent
content outside the summaries and quote files.

# Book-level distillation — brazilian-guitar-book

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Overall Arc and Pedagogical Structure

Nelson Faria's book is organized as a systematic survey of five core Brazilian guitar styles—Samba, Bossa Nova, Choro, Frevo, and Baião—each occupying its own self-contained part. The book moves from the most foundationally important rhythmic style (Samba, whose syncopation cliché is declared the single most critical figure a student must internalize) through progressively distinct regional and historical forms, ending with the northeastern Baião. The pedagogical structure is consistent throughout: each style is introduced via a core pattern, then expanded into a numbered series of variations (Samba receives seventeen; Bossa Nova four; Frevo five; Baião five) that the student is expected to learn, mix, and deploy contextually rather than apply mechanically. The book is explicitly a performance methodology, not a theoretical survey—it trains the body as much as the mind, repeatedly prescribing off-guitar exercises to build rhythmic independence before executing patterns on the instrument.

## The Master's Characteristic Method: Two-Layer Texture and Body-Percussion Training

Across every chapter, Faria's signature technical approach is the strict separation of the guitar into two independent rhythmic streams: the **thumb** anchors bass notes on the beat while **fingers 1, 2, and 3** handle the syncopated upper voices. This division of labor, introduced in Chapter 1 for Samba, is re-affirmed as the load-bearing architecture in Chapters 2, 4, and 5. The method's most characteristic pedagogical move is the **off-guitar clapping exercise**: the student claps the upper-voice rhythm and taps the lower-voice rhythm with the foot before approaching the instrument, internalizing bipartite independence kinesthetically. This body-percussion training appears explicitly in Chapters 1, 2, 3, and 5, making it a cross-style methodological constant rather than a style-specific tip.

## Bass-Line Rules and Left-Hand Prescription

Faria is unusually prescriptive about bass construction. The **root/fifth alternation rule**—with the fifth preferentially voiced *below* the root—governs bass lines in Samba (Chapter 1), Bossa Nova (Chapter 2), and Frevo (Chapter 4). Chapter 1 refines this further: when the bass operates syncopated and independently, the thumb handles it; in block-style textures, the index finger takes over instead. Chapter 4 adds that frevo bass lines keep the sixth string rooted where possible. Left-hand fingering is not left to the student's discretion: Chapter 1 specifies exact fingerings for individual variations (e.g., 3-1-4-2 for Variation #3, 4-1-1-3-2 for Variation #17) and introduces problem-solving techniques such as thumb-over and split-finger barres for wide stretches. Chapter 2 provides explicit five-note chord fingering for dense voicings. Prescription at this granularity signals that Faria treats fingering decisions as stylistically load-bearing, not merely ergonomic.

## Style-Specific Systems and Terminology

Each chapter introduces a discrete terminological vocabulary. Chapter 1's key term is the **samba syncopation cliché**, the irreducible rhythmic figure from which all seventeen variations derive, plus named sub-styles like **Partido Alto** and the **batucada** texture (which mixes syncopated and non-syncopated rhythms across four measures). Chapter 2's organizing concept is the **clave**—the rhythmic cell that must remain directionally consistent throughout a Bossa Nova performance—with variations extending from a single measure to **extended patterns** spanning two measures and employing staccato articulation or 16th-note displacement. Chapter 3 (Choro) specifies a **three-part form (A, B, C)** modulating to the V or IV key center in part C, a harmonic vocabulary of triads, seventh chords, and **inverted diminished chords**, and guitar patterns that simulate the **pandeiro** (a Brazilian frame drum). Chapter 4 names the **walking-bass analogy** (drawn explicitly from jazz) for frevo's root-fifth bass movement and introduces **marcha rancho** as a 3/4 variant treated in Variation #5. Chapter 5 identifies Baião's modal grounding in the **mixolydian and lydian b7 modes**, its simulation of **cowbell and berimbau** timbres through specific right-hand techniques, and the **pedal-tone rule** (syncopated bass against on-beat upper voices).

## Variation Deployment as Performance Technique

A rule that recurs across all five styles is that numbered variations are not simply technical exercises but **deployable performance options**. Faria explicitly states in Chapters 1, 3, 4, and 5 that variations can be mixed within a song and that pattern changes are idiomatic at **cadenzas and section endings**. This frames the entire variation system as a vocabulary of compositional gestures: the student learns not just *how* to play each pattern but *when* to switch, making contextual deployment a formal part of the method. Chapter 3 reinforces this with the observation that changing patterns at section endings is a Choro convention, not merely a pedagogical liberty. The cumulative effect across all five parts is a model of Brazilian guitar performance in which rhythmic variety within a song is achieved by cycling through a learned set of style-consistent variations rather than through improvised deviation from a single pattern.

## What the Book Teaches, Synthesized

Taken as a whole, the book teaches a **modular, body-first methodology for Brazilian rhythm guitar** in which every style reduces to (a) a foundational two-layer texture separating thumb-bass from finger-upper-voices, (b) a core rhythmic cliché or cell that must be internalized kinesthetically before being executed on the guitar, (c) a numbered set of variations derived from that cell through systematic transformation (register switching, staccato, syncopation displacement, meter change), and (d) a performance convention that treats those variations as a deployable repertoire switched at structurally meaningful moments. The harmonic language across styles is deliberately kept simple—root-fifth bass, triads and seventh chords, occasional diminished harmony—because the book's pedagogical weight falls entirely on rhythm and texture, not harmony. The student who completes all five parts will have internalized a unified physical technique (the thumb/finger split), a common practice discipline (body-percussion pre-training), and a set of style-specific rhythmic vocabularies they can apply, mix, and time with structural intention.

# Chapter 1 — PART I - Samba

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 teaches the foundational techniques and rhythmic concepts of samba guitar, beginning with the samba syncopation cliché—the single most important rhythmic figure that must become intuitive to the student. The core technical rule assigns the right hand fingers 1, 2, and 3 to syncopated upper voices while the thumb plays the on-beat bass note, a division of labor that governs all variations and which the student should practice away from the guitar by clapping high voices and tapping the low with the foot. The bass line operates under a root/fifth rule with the fifth preferentially voiced below the root, and when syncopated independently, requires the thumb for the bass line but the index finger in block-style situations—distinctions that preserve the samba feel while maintaining rhythmic independence. The chapter introduces seventeen variations (numbered #1 through #17) that systematically explore techniques including mirror-image two-measure forms, agogô simulation through voice-register switching, the batucada style that mixes syncopated and non-syncopated rhythms over four measures, and named sub-styles like Partido Alto. Left-hand fingering is prescribed specifically for each variation (such as 3-1-4-2 for Variation #3 and 4-1-1-3-2 for Variation #17), with problem-solving strategies like thumb-over technique and split-finger barres for wide stretches. The chapter establishes that variations can be mixed within a song and deployed strategically at cadenzas and section endings, teaching the student to apply patterns contextually rather than mechanically.

# Chapter 2 — PART II - Bossa Nova

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 teaches the bossa nova rhythm by grounding all right-hand patterns in the "clave," a foundational rhythmic cell that must remain directionally consistent throughout a piece, with the core technique split between fingers handling syncopated upper voices and thumb anchoring bass notes on root-fifth alternation. The chapter introduces four variations of increasing complexity—Variation #1 adds 16th-note syncopation equivalent to samba patterns, Variation #2 extends to two-measure units, Variation #3 employs staccato articulation with registral reduction, and Variation #4 serves specific performance contexts like singer's pauses with optional 16th-note displacement—each with precise fingering and harmonic-rhythmic alignment rules. Characteristic of the method's pedagogical approach, Faria prescribes off-guitar practice (clapping the bass-upper voice split) to internalize the rhythmic independence before executing it on the instrument, and provides explicit five-note chord fingering instruction for dense voicings. The load-bearing terminology introduced is "clave" (the rhythmic cell), "syncopation" (as applied to upper-voice displacement), and the concept of "extended patterns" spanning multiple measures.

# Chapter 3 — PART III - Choro

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the essential characteristics and performance practices of choro, a Brazilian style defined by 16th-note melodies in 2/4 (or 3/4 waltz) time, played by a small ensemble of flute, cavaquinho, and seven-stringed guitar with frequent soloist improvisation. The harmonic vocabulary centers on triads, seventh chords, and diminished chords typically in inverted positions, with a strong emphasis on bass-line movement, while the form follows a standard three-part structure (A, B, C) that modulates to the V or IV chord key center in part C. Guitar patterns in choro simulate pandeiro (a percussion instrument) rhythms, requiring the guitarist to coordinate high and low voices as independent rhythmic streams—a skill developed through left-hand muting practice and body-percussion exercises (hand-clapping and foot-tapping). The chapter introduces the practical flexibility of switching between multiple rhythmic variations throughout a song and the idiomatic convention of changing patterns at section endings or cadenzas, establishing variation deployment as a core performance technique in the style.

# Chapter 4 — PART IV - Frevo

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 introduces frevo, a syncopated march style that is dance-oriented, typically up tempo, and most commonly written in 4/4 time with melodic accents on the upbeat. The guitar's foundational pattern places syncopations on the top voices of the chord while the bass note (which alternates between the chord's root and fifth, preferably with the fifth below the root) lands on the beat, mirroring the "walking bass" approach of jazz. The chapter establishes that frevo bass lines keep the sixth string rooted where possible and that harmonies are simple, usually turnarounds. Faria teaches five variations that can be deployed individually throughout a song or mixed to create new patterns, with distinct rhythmic characters: Variation #1 introduces new syncopation on the third beat, Variation #4 demonstrates an up-beat-only texture (optionally executed with left-hand muting of downbeats), and Variation #5 illustrates marcha rancho in 3/4 meter. The load-bearing terminology introduced includes the frevo's syncopated rhythmic character, the bass-root-fifth alternation pattern, the walking-bass analogy, and the concept of structural variation deployment across sections and cadenzas.

# Chapter 5 — PART V - Baião

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches the foundational patterns and variations of baião, a Brazilian rhythmic style originating from the northeast (Ceará, Maranhão, and Bahia) that emerged in the 1940s from the bumba-meu-boi folk dance. The core technique centers on a two-layer texture: upper voices that simulate percussion instruments (cowbell, berimbau) against syncopated pedal-tone bass lines, all grounded in the mixolydian and lydian b7 modes with simple harmonic movement. Faria introduces five key variations—cowbell/syncopated bass, berimbau open-string technique, broken chords style, and salsa-inflected bass syncopation—each deployable either continuously through a song or mixed to create new patterns, with the characteristic load-bearing terminology being the pedal-tone rule (syncopated bass against on-beat upper voices) and the cross-training practice method of clapping high voices while tapping low voices with the foot to internalize the bipartite rhythm.

      "id": "nelson-faria:brazilian-guitar-book:style-rhythmic-cell",
      "name": "Style-Specific Rhythmic Cell System",
      "summary": "Each of the five Brazilian styles is organized around a single foundational rhythmic cell from which all variations are derived. For Samba the cell is the syncopation clich\u00e9; for Bossa Nova it is the clave (which must remain directionally fixed throughout a performance); for Choro it is the pandeiro simulation pattern; for Frevo it is the syncopated march figure with upbeat accent; for Bai\u00e3o it is the pedal-tone counterpoint. The members are these five style cells. The traversal rules govern how cells constrain variation generation and how the clave direction must hold. This system is the per-style grammar layer sitting above the universal two-layer texture.",

      "id": "nelson-faria:brazilian-guitar-book:choro-form-harmony",
      "name": "Choro Form and Harmony System",
      "summary": "Choro is the only style in the book with a prescribed macro-formal structure that governs harmonic navigation: a three-part A-B-C form in which Part C modulates to the V or IV key center. The harmonic vocabulary is also prescribed: triads, seventh chords, and inverted diminished chords, with bass-line movement treated as structurally primary. This system is smaller and more style-specific than the others\u2014it operates above the texture and variation layers and governs the large-scale harmonic trajectory of a choro performance.",
